sharedBrief:
You are advising the HAVEN/CellProtocol project on a security-sensitive authorization design. Answer in English. Do not flatter the design; disagree wherever the reasoning warrants it. State uncertainty explicitly.

CONTEXT â€” THE SYSTEM
HAVEN/CellProtocol is a local-first, permissioned data protocol. State lives in typed 'Cells'. Every mutation is a replayable flow event; the system guarantees deterministic, replayable behavior. Authority is domain-scoped identity: there is no global user/account authority. CellProtocol already has a real, signature-backed agreement layer: an `Agreement` is signed by an `Identity` (`Agreement.sign(identity:)`), carries `Grant`s (a `Grant` is a keypath + Permission primitive, e.g. read/write/execute on one keypath), and `Condition`s; a `CellResolver` enforces all contracts (checks identity validity, capability permissions, verifies conditions, rejects calls lacking permission, handles automatic revocation on expiry/condition-failure/identity-revocation). Book 04 security properties: no authority without explicit Contract; identity-bound and domain-scoped; deterministic and replayable; transparent and auditable.

CONTEXT â€” CO-PILOT CHAT AND THE ESCALATION CEILING
Co-Pilot Chat is the central GUI. When a user types a draft, a local-rules interpreter maps it to structured intent. A 'model ladder' exists (local rules -> on-device -> scoped RAG -> external API -> agent bridge). The product wants an interpreter ceiling: how high on the ladder interpretation may automatically escalate WITHOUT a per-message click. The stated V1 default is 'automatic within grant'. Six binding shipping conditions are already agreed and, except for the grant issuer, already implemented and tested as a DECISION-ONLY engine (it decides but performs no external call):
1. Interpretation only â€” automatic applies to lane selection for interpretation; all side effects still require a click.
2. Explicit automatic semantics in the grant â€” a grant to use an external model ON CLICK is NOT a grant to use it AUTOMATICALLY; escalation_mode is bound to provider kind, model class, cell scope, purpose (draft interpretation), data classes, duration, quota.
3. A deterministic LOCAL pre-flight PII/data-class gate runs on every draft before any automatic escalation; flagged content downgrades that message to ask-this-time regardless of grant.
4. Layered clamping, downward only â€” effective ceiling = most restrictive of {Entity base config, scaffold layer, Perspective, active grant}; layers can restrict, never widen; conference scope clamps to ask-each-time.
5. Audit before automation â€” every routing decision emits model.route.* flow events (recommended/invoked/denied/completed/failed) with a shared correlationId; no decision is silent.
6. Cost/quota policy with a visible meter plus a per-conversation kill switch; exhaustion or pause forces downgrade to ask-this-time.
RED LINES (non-negotiable): no hidden remote model call; no blanket AI consent; no private keys/secrets/tokens in flow payloads; interpretation output is a PROPOSAL that never enters cell state without a click.

CONTEXT â€” THE GAP AND THE OWNER'S DECISION
The decision engine today consumes an escalation grant that is a plain, UNSIGNED, ISSUER-LESS data struct (it has an id and a duration but no issuer, no signature, no persistence, no identity binding â€” any code could fabricate one). This is safe only because no live external-call path exists yet. Before wiring an actual external model call, the grant needs a REAL ISSUER. The project owner has already decided the issuer is ONLY THE USER THEMSELVES, self-signing a grant scoped to their own Entity â€” there is no third-party issuer, no admin, no organization, no delegation chain. Consequence to reckon with: the grant therefore does not protect against the user (who can always widen their own ceiling); it is a consent record + bound policy + revocable kill lever that protects against the SOFTWARE escalating without a deliberate, signed, revocable, audited act of the user, and against a malicious or buggy cell/process fabricating a grant.

QUESTIONS â€” answer each, numbered, decisively.
Q1. ARCHITECTURE FORK (A vs B). Option A: make the escalation grant a first-class CellProtocol `Agreement` with `Grant`s over specific keypaths plus a new `Condition` type encoding escalation_mode/provider/model-class/data-classes/quota/cost; inherits signature, identity-binding, domain-scoping, automatic revocation, replay-determinism, resolver enforcement for free, but requires changing high-guardrail core agreement/condition machinery. Option B: keep the rich escalation-grant descriptor as a domain policy object, but make it signed by the user's Identity, stored owner-scoped in their Entity, with issuance and revocation being resolver-gated writes on the chat cell, and the engine verifying the signature before trusting it â€” no core change, but a two-layer trust model. Which should HAVEN adopt for a PRODUCTION-READY system, and why? If B, state exactly which security properties of A must be re-established by hand and how. If A, state the minimum viable Condition contract and whether it is worth the core-change risk.
Q2. GRANT OWNERSHIP LAYER. Should the grant be owned at the Entity-base layer (applies across all of the user's chat scaffolds) or as a per-scaffold instance (a separate grant per scaffold, e.g. personal co-pilot vs conference)? Analyze blast radius, the downward-only clamp interaction with conference scope, revocation ergonomics, and the user's mental model. Recommend a default.
Q3. GRANT DURATION. Session-only vs persistent-with-expiry, and if persistent, the default expiry. Weigh consent freshness and the risk of silent re-escalation weeks later against consent fatigue and friction. Recommend a concrete default duration and expiry/renewal behavior, and say what must happen on expiry (hard downgrade to ask, silent renewal never).
Q4. SELF-ISSUED THREAT MODEL. Given the issuer is the user's own identity self-signing a self-scoped grant, what does the signature ACTUALLY protect against, and is self-signing meaningful or security theater? Identify which forgery/replay/confused-deputy protections remain load-bearing (e.g. a compromised cell fabricating a descriptor, replaying an expired grant, or widening scope) and the minimum mechanisms that make the self-issued grant trustworthy.
Q5. REPLAY/DETERMINISM RECONCILIATION. A grant that authorizes a NON-deterministic, cost-bearing external model call sits in tension with Book 04's deterministic+replayable guarantee, and with 'no side effect without a click' (an automatic external call IS a cost-bearing network side effect the grant pre-authorizes). How should HAVEN reconcile this? Specify what must be recorded as flow, what must stay proposal-only, how the external output is kept replay-neutral, and how quota is decremented deterministically.
Q6. PRODUCTION HARDENING. Specify the minimal but genuinely production-ready issuer design: the issuance consent surface (what the user must see about what leaves scope, model class, quota/cost cap, duration before signing), one-click revocation, quota accounting, provider-credential handling (never in the grant or flow), failure behavior (provider down -> downgrade to ask, never silent fail), and the audit chain that ties every automatic call to a specific signed grant id issued by the user at a time. What is the smallest robust build?
Q7. VERDICT. One decisive paragraph: your recommendation on Q1/Q2/Q3, and whether to split the work into sub-slice A (real issuer + signature + storage + revocation + quota accounting, still NO live external call, fully testable) followed by sub-slice B (wire exactly one real external lane behind the verified grant). Go or no-go on that split.

FORMAT
Numbered answers Q1-Q7. Maximum ~1400 words total. Cite established security/authorization concepts where relevant (capability security, macaroons/caveats, OAuth scopes, POLA, confused deputy, replay nonces) by name. Be concrete enough that an engineer could build from your answer. Disagree with the owner's framing wherever warranted.

results.responseText:
**Q1. ARCHITECTURE FORK (A vs B)**

Adopt **B**, but treat it as a deliberately constrained subset of A, not a parallel reimplementation. The core reason is risk asymmetry: CellProtocol's `Agreement`/`Condition` machinery is explicitly high-guardrail, deterministic, replay-backed, and security-load-bearing across the entire protocol. Adding an escalation-specific `Condition` type (provider kind, model class, data-class gate, quota, cost cap, purpose binding) into that core means every future change to the Co-Pilot ladder ripples into the core contract surface â€” and the escalation domain is the *least* stable part of this system (providers, model classes, pricing, PII rules will churn). Coupling volatile product policy to the most safety-critical primitive in the system is the wrong direction of the dependency arrow.

But B is only acceptable if it does **not** re-implement agreement machinery badly. The properties A gives for free that B must re-establish by hand:

- **Signature binding**: the descriptor is signed by the user's `Identity`; verification is mandatory before any routing decision trusts it. No unsigned struct ever reaches the decision engine's "trust" path.
- **Domain scoping**: the descriptor is bound to a specific Entity id and (per Q2) scaffold id; a descriptor issued for Entity X / scaffold Y is rejected in any other context. This is the capability-security "confinement" property â€” the capability cannot be replayed into a wider scope.
- **Caveat enforcement (macaroon model)**: each field in the descriptor (provider, model class, data classes, quota, duration, purpose) is a *caveat*, not a hint. The engine must refuse any escalation that violates any caveat; caveats are intersected (downward-only), never unioned.
- **Revocation**: revocation is a resolver-gated write that invalidates the descriptor id; the decision engine must check revocation status on every routing decision, not cache "trusted" across messages.
- **Replay protection**: the descriptor carries an issuance timestamp and expiry; expired descriptors are rejected. A per-message nonce binds a specific routing decision to a specific descriptor version so a stale "completed" event cannot be replayed as authority for a new call.
- **Audit binding**: every `model.route.*` event must carry the descriptor id + signature fingerprint + the caveat snapshot in force at decision time.

The one property B cannot inherit is *uniform enforcement location*: A would force all checks through `CellResolver`; B requires the Co-Pilot engine to call into a verifier discipline it must maintain. That is the real cost of B, and it must be owned explicitly in code review gates, not assumed.

**Q2. GRANT OWNERSHIP LAYER**

Default to **per-scaffold instance**, with Entity-base as an optional *template* the user can instantiate from, not a live grant. Reasons:

- **Blast radius**: an Entity-base grant that permits automatic external escalation applies to every scaffold the user ever creates, including a conference scaffold where the data is demonstrably not theirs alone. The downward-only clamp is supposed to handle this, but clamps are a mitigation, not a boundary â€” a clamp bug or a misconfigured scaffold leaks a base grant into a context it was never reasoned about. Per-scaffold issuance makes the boundary structural.
- **Conference scope**: conference scaffolds must clamp to ask-each-time anyway (condition 4). If the default grant is Entity-base, the conference path is *always* the clamped exception, which means the base grant is tuned for the non-conference case and the conference case is a permanent special case. Per-scaffold makes each scaffold's grant honest about its own scope.
- **Revocation ergonomics**: per-scaffold means revoking a grant kills exactly one scaffold's automatic escalation without collateral. Entity-base revocation is a sledgehammer; per-scaffold is a scalpel. Users will want the scalpel.
- **Mental model**: "this Co-Pilot can auto-escalate; that one can't" is the model users actually hold. "My whole identity permits auto-escalation but each Co-Pilot may clamp it" is a capability-security-correct model that most users will not reason about correctly under pressure.

Entity-base should exist only as a *prefill* at scaffold creation: "apply your default preferences," producing a fresh signed per-scaffold grant. The Entity-base template itself is never a live authority.

**Q3. GRANT DURATION**

**Persistent with expiry; default 7 days; hard downgrade on expiry; no silent renewal, ever.**

- Session-only is the safest but produces consent fatigue on every cold start, which drives users to click "allow everything" patterns â€” the exact failure mode the red lines forbid. Session-only is the wrong default for a daily-use tool.
- Persistent-without-expiry is the silent-re-escalation risk: a user who authorized external API escalation in week 1 and forgot about it is effectively under blanket consent in week 6. That violates the spirit of "no blanket AI consent" even if it technically has a revocable grant.
- 7 days is the sweet spot: long enough that daily use doesn't re-prompt, short enough that a forgotten grant self-disables before it becomes ambient. It also aligns with a weekly review cadence a user might actually perform.

On expiry: **hard downgrade to ask-this-time for every message until the user signs a new grant.** No silent renewal, no "continue with last settings" prompt that pre-ticks the old values â€” the renewal surface must force a fresh, explicit view of provider/model-class/data-classes/quota/cost/duration, same as initial issuance. A "renew with same terms" one-click is acceptable *only if* the full terms are displayed alongside the click, not hidden behind it.

Uncertainty: I am not confident 7 days is right for all user populations. A power user may want 30; a high-sensitivity user may want per-session. Make it user-selectable with 7 days as the default, and never offer "never expire."

**Q4. SELF-ISSUED THREAT MODEL**

The owner's framing is correct on one point and incomplete on another. Correct: a self-issued grant does not protect against the user, and *should not pretend to*. The user is the principal; the principal can always widen their own authority. That is not a flaw, it is the definition of principal authority.

Incomplete: the signature is **not security theater** â€” it is load-bearing against three concrete threats, and the design fails if any of these is not handled:

1. **Fabrication by a compromised/malicious cell or process**: without a signature, any code that can write to the chat cell can mint a grant with `escalation_mode: automatic, provider: external_api, data_classes: all`. The signature makes fabrication require the user's private key, which a cell does not have. This is the primary protection. *Minimum mechanism*: the decision engine verifies signature against the user's `Identity` public key on every routing decision; unsigned or mis-signed descriptors are rejected and logged as `model.route.denied` with reason `invalid_signature`.
2. **Replay of an expired or revoked grant**: a stolen descriptor blob should not be reusable after expiry/revocation. *Minimum mechanism*: descriptor carries `issued_at`, `expires_at`, `id`; engine checks both plus a revocation flag on every decision. Per-message nonce in the flow event prevents replaying a *completed* event as authority for a new call.
3. **Confused deputy**: the Co-Pilot engine is the deputy; without binding the descriptor to a specific Entity+scaffold+purpose, a routing path could apply a grant intended for "draft interpretation" to a different purpose, or a grant for scaffold A to scaffold B. *Minimum mechanism*: descriptor binds Entity id, scaffold id, purpose ("draft interpretation"), and the engine refuses any mismatch. This is the OAuth-scope-binding discipline.

What the signature does **not** protect against: the user themselves widening scope, a malicious process that has already stolen the user's private key (game over regardless), or a process that can intercept the click stream and trick the user into signing a wider grant than they intended (the issuance surface, Q6, is the mitigation).

**Q5. REPLAY/DETERMINISM RECONCILIATION**

This is a genuine tension and the owner has under-articulated it. An automatic external call is a cost-bearing, non-deterministic network side effect. Book 04's "deterministic and replayable" guarantee cannot honestly cover the call itself. The reconciliation is: **the external call is outside the deterministic replay boundary; everything around it is inside.**

Concretely:

- **What is recorded as flow**: the *decision* (`model.route.recommended`, `model.route.invoked` with descriptor id + signature fingerprint + caveat snapshot + correlationId), the *request intent hash* (a deterministic hash of the interpreted intent + the data-class gate result + the caveat snapshot â€” not the raw draft, which may contain PII), the *response arrival* (`model.route.completed` with a response hash, not the raw response), and the *failure* (`model.route.failed` with reason). The raw draft and raw response are **not** in the flow event payload; they live in the chat cell's proposal staging area, which is already click-gated for state entry.
- **What stays proposal-only**: the external model's output is a proposal, same as any interpretation output. It does not enter cell state without a click. This is already a red line; the reconciliation is that the *flow event records the proposal's existence and hash*, not the proposal's content.
- **Replay-neutral external output**: on replay, the engine must **not** re-invoke the external model. Replay reads the recorded `model.route.completed` event, retrieves the staged proposal by hash, and re-presents it. The external call is a one-time side effect that occurred in the original execution; replay is a *re-presentation* of its recorded outcome, not a re-performance. This is the same discipline as replaying a UI event: the side effect happened once; replay reconstructs state from the event log, not by re-triggering the side effect.
- **Quota decrement**: quota is decremented at `model.route.invoked` (the moment the call is committed), not at completion or click. On replay, the quota event is already in the log; replay does not decrement again. Quota accounting is therefore deterministic *because it is event-log-derived*, not because the external call is deterministic. A failed call still decrements (the cost was incurred); a denied call does not.

Uncertainty: I am not certain the "request intent hash" is sufficient for audit without the raw draft. If audit needs to reconstruct *what was sent*, the raw draft must be stored somewhere â€” but not in the flow payload, and not in a way that violates the PII gate. This needs a separate decision: an encrypted, cell-scoped audit vault, or accept that audit is hash-only. I lean hash-only for V1 and revisit if forensics demand it.

**Q6. PRODUCTION HARDENING**

Smallest robust build:

- **Issuance consent surface**: a modal (not a toast, not a banner) that displays, before signing: provider name + model class; exact data classes that may leave scope (and the PII gate's verdict on the current draft if available); purpose ("draft interpretation only"); quota cap (e.g., "50 automatic calls / 24h"); cost cap (e.g., "$2/day"); duration (default 7 days, user-selectable); scaffold id and Entity id being bound; a plain-language line: "This permits automatic external model calls without per-message confirmation, within the limits above. Revoke at any time." The user signs with their Identity. No pre-ticked "remember this" â€” the signature *is* the remembering.
- **One-click revocation**: a persistent, visible control in the Co-Pilot surface showing the active grant's remaining quota, expiry, and a "Revoke" button. Revocation is a resolver-gated write that flips the descriptor's revocation flag; the decision engine checks the flag on every routing decision. Revocation is immediate, not deferred to session end.
- **Quota accounting**: a counter in the chat cell, decremented at `model.route.invoked`, derived from the event log (not a separate mutable int). Quota reset is time-windowed (rolling 24h), not session-bound. Exhaustion emits `model.route.denied` with reason `quota_exhausted` and forces ask-this-time.
- **Provider credentials**: never in the grant, never in the flow payload, never in the cell. Credentials live in a separate credential store keyed by provider+Entity, accessed by the *external-call adapter* (sub-slice B) at invocation time only. The grant references the provider by id; the adapter resolves idâ†’credential at call time. This is the OAuth token-vs-scope separation.
- **Failure behavior**: provider down, timeout, auth failure â†’ `model.route.failed`, downgrade to ask-this-time for that message, never silent retry, never silent fallback to a different provider. The user sees the failure and clicks to retry or picks a different lane.
- **Audit chain**: every `model.route.*` event carries `descriptor_id`, `descriptor_signature_fingerprint`, `caveat_snapshot` (the effective ceiling at decision time, post-clamp), `correlationId`, `entity_id`, `scaffold_id`. An auditor can reconstruct, for any automatic call: which grant authorized it, who signed that grant, when, what caveats were in force, and which message triggered it.

**Q7. VERDICT**

Adopt **B** (signed, per-scaffold, 7-day-expiry, self-issued descriptor verified by the engine, with macaroon-style caveats and explicit re-establishment of signature binding, domain scoping, revocation, replay protection, and audit binding by hand), because coupling volatile escalation policy to CellProtocol's core `Agreement` machinery is a dependency-direction error not worth the core-change risk. Default the grant to **per-scaffold instance** with Entity-base as a prefill template only, because blast radius and the conference-clamp interaction make Entity-base grants structurally fragile. Default duration to **7 days persistent with hard expiry to ask-this-time and no silent renewal**, because consent freshness matters more than friction reduction and "never expire" is a blanket-consent failure mode in slow motion. **Go on the split**: sub-slice A (real issuer + signature + storage + revocation + quota accounting + full audit chain, still no live external call, fully testable as a decision-only engine with a mock provider adapter) is the right shape â€” it forces the trust model to be real before any network side effect exists, and it makes sub-slice B (wire exactly one real external lane behind the verified grant) a small, reviewable diff rather than a simultaneous trust-and-wiring change. The one risk in the split is that A's mock adapter hides integration failures that only B exposes; mitigate by making the mock adapter's interface identical to the real adapter's, so B is a credential-and-endpoint swap, not a redesign.

Here is my assessment and recommendation, speaking specifically from the perspective of replay-determinism, auditability, and capability security.

**Q1. ARCHITECTURE FORK (A vs B)**
I recommend **Option A**. You should make the escalation grant a first-class CellProtocol `Agreement`. 
While Option B avoids touching core machinery, it forces you to manually rebuild a parallel capability-security layer (signature verification, expiration, revocation, and scope binding). This violates the architectural integrity of HAVEN. If you use Option B, you are essentially building a bespoke, poorly-tested Macaroon/caveat system on top of a protocol that already has a robust one. 
By choosing Option A, the AI escalation becomes a standard capability. The minimum viable `Condition` contract must include: `provider_kind`, `model_class`, `allowed_data_classes`, `max_quota_value`, and `expiry_timestamp`. The core-change risk is worth it because it guarantees that if a grant expires or is revoked, the `CellResolver` automatically drops the capability at the protocol level, making it mathematically impossible for the routing engine to execute the call.

**Q2. GRANT OWNERSHIP LAYER**
The grant should be owned at the **Entity-base layer**, not per-scaffold. 
*Blast Radius & Mental Model:* Users view their relationship with an AI provider (e.g., "I allow my local system to send data to OpenAI") as a global preference, not a per-room setting. Forcing per-scaffold grants causes severe consent fatigue.
*Clamp Interaction:* The downward-only clamping mechanism (Condition 4) perfectly mitigates the blast radius of an Entity-base grant. If the user has a global grant for automatic escalation, but enters a highly sensitive "Conference" scaffold, the scaffold's local Perspective clamps the ceiling down to "ask-each-time." This provides the best of both worlds: ergonomic global consent with strict, context-aware local overrides.

**Q3. GRANT DURATION**
I recommend **persistent-with-expiry**, specifically a default of **7 days**. 
Session-only grants generate too much friction, training users to blindly click "Approve" (consent fatigue), which defeats the purpose of the gate. However, indefinite persistence risks silent re-escalation weeks later when the user has forgotten they granted automatic access. 
Upon expiry, the system must execute a **hard downgrade to ask-this-time**. Silent renewal is an absolute anti-pattern. The user must be presented with a new consent surface to issue a new grant.

**Q4. SELF-ISSUED THREAT MODEL**
Self-signing is not security theater; it is a textbook defense against the **Confused Deputy** problem. 
Even though the user is issuing the grant to themselves, the signature protects the user from *the software*. Without a cryptographic signature, a buggy UI component, a compromised local cell, or a malicious plugin could fabricate a plain JSON object claiming "the user allows automatic GPT-4 calls" and drain the user's quota or leak data. 
The signature ensures that the escalation capability was explicitly minted by the user's private key. Load-bearing protections include:
1.  **Signature verification:** Proves user intent.
2.  **Nonce/Grant ID:** Prevents replay attacks (re-using a revoked grant).
3.  **Strict scope binding:** Ensures the grant cannot be widened by the software after issuance.

**Q5. REPLAY/DETERMINISM RECONCILIATION**
This is the most critical architectural tension. A deterministic, replayable system cannot natively house a non-deterministic, cost-bearing network call in its state mutations. You must separate the *intent and receipt* from the *side effect*.
Here is how you reconcile it:
1.  **Flow Events (The Audit Trail):** The flow must record `model.route.invoked` (containing the Grant ID, prompt hash, and requested model) and `model.route.completed` (containing the provider's response hash, exact token usage, and cost). 
2.  **Proposal-Only State:** The actual text generated by the AI *never* enters the Cell's core state. It is held in an ephemeral "Proposal" state. It only becomes a deterministic state mutation (a standard `MessageCreated` event) if and when the user clicks "Accept".
3.  **Replay-Neutrality:** During a state replay, the system reads the `model.route.invoked` and `model.route.completed` events. Because the system is in replay mode, the external call is **bypassed entirely**. The system simply restores the Proposal state using the payload referenced in the `completed` event. 
4.  **Deterministic Quota Accounting:** Quota is decremented deterministically by the `CellResolver` processing the `model.route.completed` flow event. Because flow events are strictly ordered and replayable, the quota calculation remains perfectly deterministic across all nodes.

**Q6. PRODUCTION HARDENING**
The minimal robust build requires a strict audit chain:
*   **Consent Surface:** The UI must explicitly state: "You are allowing [App] to automatically send [Data Classes] to [Provider/Model] for the next [7 Days], up to a cost of [X Quota]."
*   **Revocation:** A one-click "Revoke" button that writes a `GrantRevoked` flow event, immediately invalidating the capability in the `CellResolver`.
*   **Credentials:** API keys or bearer tokens live strictly in the local secure enclave/keychain. They are injected at the network boundary and *never* touch the grant, the flow events, or the Cell state.
*   **Failure Behavior:** If the provider is down or times out, the system emits `model.route.failed` and immediately downgrades the current interaction to "ask-this-time".
*   **The Audit Chain:** Every automatic call must be traceable: `User Signature (Grant ID)` -> `model.route.invoked (references Grant ID)` -> `Network Call` -> `model.route.completed (records cost)` -> `Proposal State` -> `User Click` -> `Cell Mutation`.

**Q7. VERDICT**
Adopt Option A (first-class Agreement), Entity-base ownership with scaffold clamping, and a 7-day persistent expiry. 
**Go on the split.** You absolutely must split this work. Sub-slice A (building the real issuer, signature verification, storage, revocation, and deterministic quota accounting via flow events) is highly complex and must be tested in isolation as a decision-only engine. Only once Sub-slice A is mathematically proven to reject unauthorized or expired routing intents should you proceed to Sub-slice B (wiring the actual external network lane). Mixing the capability-security overhaul with live network side-effects in a single PR is a recipe for a critical security regression.

Q1. **Adopt Option A.** The system already has a production-hardened capability model: `Agreement` carries signed `Grant`s over keypaths, `Condition`s are evaluated deterministically by the `CellResolver`, and revocation is automatic on expiry, condition failure, or identity revocation. Introducing a parallel trust model (Option B) would require reimplementing signature verification, replay protection, revocation, and scope enforcementâ€”exactly the machinery that already exists and is tested. That duplication is a source of divergence and bugs. The â€œhigh-guardrail core changeâ€‌ is modest: add a new `Condition` type (e.g., `EscalationCondition`) that encodes `escalation_mode`, `provider_kind`, `model_class`, `cell_scope`, `purpose`, `data_classes`, `duration`, `quota`. The resolver already dispatches on condition type; adding one is a low-risk extension. The minimum viable condition contract must be a pure function of the conditionâ€™s fields and the current cell state (quota remaining, current time) so that evaluation remains deterministic and replayable. The condition should also reference a specific grant ID to enable audit tying. The benefit is that the grant inherits identity binding, domain scoping, automatic revocation, and replay-determinism for free. The risk of core change is outweighed by the security guarantees gained.

Q2. **Per-scaffold ownership is the safer default.** A grant scoped to the Entity base would apply across all scaffolds, including future ones the user might create. While the conference scaffoldâ€™s downward-only clamp to ask-each-time prevents automatic escalation there, a per-scaffold grant limits blast radius: if a grant is inadvertently too permissive or a scaffold is compromised, only that scaffold is affected. Revocation ergonomics are betterâ€”revoking a scaffoldâ€™s grant doesnâ€™t disrupt other workflows. The userâ€™s mental model aligns with â€œIâ€™m allowing this chat to use automatic suggestions,â€‌ not â€œIâ€™m allowing all chats everywhere.â€‌ The system can still offer an Entity-wide grant as an advanced option, but the default should be per-scaffold. The downward-only clamp interaction is clean: the effective ceiling for a scaffold is the most restrictive of the scaffoldâ€™s grant, the Entity base config, and any Perspective layer; a per-scaffold grant simply adds another layer that can only restrict further.

Q3. **Session-only with an explicit opt-in for longer durations.** Consent freshness is critical because automatic escalation authorizes cost-bearing external calls that may leak data. A persistent grant with a long expiry (e.g., 30 days) risks silent re-escalation weeks later when the user has forgotten the grant. Session-only (grant expires when the session ends or after a short idle timeout) forces periodic reaffirmation without excessive fatigue if the session is active. For power users, offer a â€œremember for 24 hoursâ€‌ or â€œthis weekâ€‌ option, but never silently renew. On expiry, the engine must hard-downgrade to ask-this-time for the next draft; no automatic renewal. The grant UI must show a visible countdown and a one-click â€œextendâ€‌ action that re-issues a new signed grant. This balances security and usability.

Q4. **Self-signing is not theater; it provides integrity and origin authentication.** The signature proves that the userâ€™s identity (the private key holder) deliberately created this exact policy at a specific time. Without it, any code running in the userâ€™s process could fabricate a grant descriptor. The signature binds the grantâ€™s parameters (scope, quota, duration, etc.) to the userâ€™s intent. The load-bearing protections:
- **Forgery resistance:** A compromised cell or malicious dependency cannot create a valid grant because it lacks the private key. The engine must verify the signature against the userâ€™s public identity before trusting the grant.
- **Replay protection:** The grant must include a unique, unpredictable `grant_id` (a nonce or UUID) and be stored as a revocable object. The engine checks that the grant is still active (not revoked, not expired) before each use. If the grant is an `Agreement` (Option A), the resolver already enforces this; otherwise, a revocation list must be maintained.
- **Confused-deputy defense:** The grant must be scoped to a specific cell, scaffold, purpose, and data classes. The engine verifies that the current escalation request matches the grantâ€™s scope exactly. The signature covers these fields, so a malicious cell cannot widen the scope by presenting a grant intended for a different context.
- **Binding to the userâ€™s Entity:** The grant is signed by the userâ€™s identity and stored in their Entity. The engine must reject grants signed by other identities, preventing cross-user confusion.
The minimum trustworthy mechanism: a signed, immutable grant object with a unique ID, stored in the userâ€™s Entity, with the engine verifying the signature, checking revocation status, and matching scope on every use.

Q5. **Reconcile by separating the deterministic decision from the non-deterministic side effect.** The automatic escalation *decision* is deterministic: the engine evaluates the grant, the pre-flight PII gate, and the layered clamps, then emits a `model.route.invoked` flow event with a correlation ID. That event is part of the replayable log. The actual external model call is a side effect that is *not* replayed. During replay, the system encounters the `model.route.invoked` event and knows a call was made, but it does not re-execute the call. Instead, it can either skip the call (since its output was only a proposal) or use a cached response if available for debugging, but the proposal never enters cell state without a click. Quota decrement must be deterministic: when the decision is made, a `model.quota.consumed` event is emitted and the quota counter in the cell state is updated. Replay replays that event, ensuring the quota state is consistent. The external output is kept replay-neutral by never being stored as cell state; only the fact of invocation and its correlation ID are recorded. This design respects the â€œno side effect without a clickâ€‌ red line because the automatic call is pre-authorized by a deliberate, signed grantâ€”the click happened at grant issuance time. The grant is a capability that authorizes a class of side effects, similar to how OAuth tokens pre-authorize API calls.

Q6. **Minimal production-ready issuer design:**
- **Issuance consent surface:** A clear dialog showing exactly what will be allowed: data classes that may leave the device, model class and provider, maximum cost/quota per call and aggregate cap, duration, and the specific scaffold/cell scope. The user must explicitly sign with their identity (e.g., biometric or passphrase). The dialog must not use dark patterns; the default button should be â€œCancel,â€‌ not â€œAllow.â€‌
- **One-click revocation:** A list of active grants in settings, each with a â€œRevokeâ€‌ button. Revocation writes a `grant.revoked` event (or updates the Agreement state to revoked), and the resolver immediately denies further automatic escalations using that grant.
- **Quota accounting:** A deterministic counter in the chat cellâ€™s state, decremented by `model.quota.consumed` events. The counter is checked before each automatic call; if exhausted, the engine downgrades to ask-this-time. A visible meter in the UI shows remaining quota. The kill switch (pause) sets the counter to zero or a separate `paused` flag, forcing downgrade.
- **Provider-credential handling:** Credentials (API keys, tokens) are stored in a secure system keychain or enclave, bound to the userâ€™s identity. They are never included in flow events or the grant. The engine retrieves them at call time using the provider kind from the grant, and only if the grant is valid.
- **Failure behavior:** If the provider is unreachable or returns an error, the engine emits `model.route.failed` with the error and correlation ID, then falls back to ask-this-time (or a lower lane if available). It must never silently fail or retry without user awareness.
- **Audit chain:** Every automatic call is linked to the grant ID that authorized it. Flow events: `model.route.recommended` (decision), `model.route.invoked` (call started), `model.route.completed` or `model.route.failed`, and `model.quota.consumed` all share a `correlationId` and include the `grant_id`. This creates a tamper-evident log.
- **Smallest robust build:** Implement the grant as an `Agreement` with an `EscalationCondition`; build the issuance UI and revocation; add quota accounting as a cell state variable updated by flow events; implement the engineâ€™s signature verification and scope matching; use the system keychain for credentials; emit all audit events. This is sub-slice A: fully testable without any live external call. Sub-slice B then wires one real external lane behind the verified grant.

Q7. **Verdict:** Adopt Option A (first-class `Agreement` with a new `Condition`), default to per-scaffold grants with session-only duration and an explicit opt-in for longer, and split the work into sub-slice A (issuer, signature, storage, revocation, quota accounting, audit events, all testable without external calls) followed by sub-slice B (wire one real lane). This approach leverages the existing capability security model, minimizes new attack surface, and ensures that the automatic escalation feature is built on a foundation of verifiable user consent, deterministic auditing, and revocable authority. Go on that split.

**Q1.** Adopt **Option A**. Building a one-off signature verifier in the engine (Option B) creates a shadow authorization layer that will drift from the resolver and reintroduces confused-deputy risk; it violates POLA by giving the engine discretionary authority outside the Contract layer. The Book 04 guaranteeâ€”"no authority without explicit Contract"â€”is meaningless if the escalation ceiling is enforced by an ad-hoc struct. Model the policy as a macaroon-style `EscalationCaveat` attached to a Grant on keypath `/system/copilot/escalation` with Permission `auto`. The caveat payload carries `escalation_mode`, `provider_kind`, `model_class`, `data_classes`, `purpose`, `quota_budget`, `cost_ceiling`, and `expiry`. The resolver validates signature,
